1:
"And there was given me a reed like unto a rod: and the angel stood, saying, Rise, and measure the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein." The witnesses note the command to measure the temple, the altar, and the worshippers. On "there was given me a reed like unto a rod," the grammatical voices note the measuring instrument. Barnes and Robertson note the symbol: "there was given me a reed like unto a rod (a measuring-reed): and the angel stood, saying, Rise, and measure" — John is given a measuring-reed and commanded to measure; the act of measuring (drawn from Ezekiel 40-43; Zechariah 2) signifies marking out, claiming, or preserving what is measured.

On "measure the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein," interpreters note the meaning. Barnes and Ellicott note that measuring "the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein" signifies the preservation and recognition of the true church and its true worshippers; in the symbolic reading, the temple is the church (not the literal building, which was likely already destroyed); the measuring sets apart and protects the genuine people of God.

On the temple, the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede note the church-reading: the temple is the church; the measuring marks out the true worshippers within it, distinguishing them from the false (the outer court, v.2); the patristic and idealist reading takes the whole vision of the church's preservation amid persecution. The verse displays the command to measure the temple, the altar, and the worshippers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the measuring — "a reed like unto a rod... Rise, and measure the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein," John given a measuring-reed to mark out the temple and worshippers (Ezekiel 40, Barnes, Robertson); measuring signifying the preservation of the true church and worshippers, the temple the church (Barnes, Ellicott); the measuring distinguishing the true from the false (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the measured temple. The verse marks the reed: like a rod. The verse displays the command: measure the temple. The verse displays the altar: and worshippers. The verse displays the meaning: preserving the true church. The command displays the true worshippers marked out and preserved. The verse displays the reed: given. The verse displays the temple: measured. The verse displays the worshippers: counted. The command to measure the temple, the altar, and the worshippers displays the opening of the chapter: John is given a reed and bidden to measure the temple of God, the altar, and the worshippers — the true church and its genuine worshippers marked out and preserved. The verse displays the measured sanctuary, over against the trodden outer court (v.2).
2:
"But the court which is without the temple leave out, and measure it not; for it is given unto the Gentiles: and the holy city shall they tread under foot forty and two months." The witnesses note the outer court given to the Gentiles and the city trodden forty-two months — the crux of the prophetic period. On "the court which is without the temple leave out, and measure it not; for it is given unto the Gentiles," the grammatical voices note the unmeasured court. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "the court which is without (the outer court) leave out (cast out), and measure it not; for it is given unto the Gentiles (the nations)" — the outer court is not measured, but abandoned to the Gentiles; in the symbolic reading, the outward, corrupted, or apostate part is given over to the profane, while the true sanctuary is preserved.

On "the holy city shall they tread under foot forty and two months," interpreters divide over the period. Barnes (with the historicist tradition) holds that "forty and two months" (42 × 30 = 1260 days) is to be reckoned by the day-year principle (Daniel 7:25; Ezekiel 4:6) as 1260 years — the long period of the treading down of the church by the apostate/papal power; the same period as the 1260 days (v.3), the "time, times and half a time," and the 42 months elsewhere. The preterist/literal reading (Stuart) takes the 42 months as a literal three years and a half — applied to the Roman siege and treading of Jerusalem, or to a literal future period; the futurist takes it as a literal final 3.5 years. The verse displays the outer court given to the Gentiles and the city trodden forty-two months.

The witnesses agree the substance: the outer court is given up to the Gentiles while the sanctuary is preserved, and the holy city is trodden underfoot for the appointed forty-two months (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). But the period divides them: the historicist day-year reading of 1260 years (Barnes, the Protestant tradition) vs the literal three-and-a-half years (preterist/futurist, Stuart). The verse displays the prophetic-period crux. The verse marks the court: unmeasured, given to the Gentiles. The verse displays the city: trodden. The verse displays the debate: 1260 years or 3.5 years. The verse displays the period: forty-two months. The vision displays the church trodden for the appointed time. The verse displays the court: cast out. The verse displays the city: trodden. The verse displays the months: forty-two. The outer court given to the Gentiles and the city trodden forty-two months display the period crux: the outer court is abandoned to the nations and the holy city trodden for forty-two months — read by the historicists as 1260 years of the church's oppression (the day-year principle, Barnes) and by the preterist/futurist as a literal three and a half years (Stuart). The verse displays the trodden city, the time of the two witnesses' prophecy (v.3).
3:
"And I will give power unto my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy a thousand two hundred and threescore days, clothed in sackcloth." The witnesses note the two witnesses prophesying 1260 days in sackcloth — the crux of their identity. On "I will give power unto my two witnesses," interpreters divide over who the witnesses are. The Reformed Gill (and the historicist tradition) hold the "two witnesses" are not literal individuals but the faithful ministers and churches who witnessed for Christ and against antichrist through the long apostasy — the Waldenses and Piedmont churches especially fitting the character; the witnessing church kept alive amid the darkness. The latin-fathers and patristic tradition (noted by Gill) held they are two literal prophets — Enoch and Elijah (or Moses and Elijah), to come before Christ's return and oppose antichrist (the futurist/patristic reading, which the signs of vv.5-6 — fire, drought, water to blood — echo); Gill notes and rejects this. A third view (also noted and rejected by Gill) takes them as the two Testaments, Old and New, witnessing to Christ.

On "they shall prophesy a thousand two hundred and threescore days, clothed in sackcloth," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the period and dress. Barnes and Henry note that the witnesses prophesy for "a thousand two hundred and threescore days (1260 days, the same period as the 42 months, v.2, read by historicists as 1260 years), clothed in sackcloth" — the sackcloth marks their mourning and the affliction of their testimony during the long period of the church's oppression; few yet sufficient witnesses, testifying in mourning.

The witnesses agree the substance: Christ raises up two witnesses who prophesy throughout the 1260-day period in the sackcloth of affliction (Barnes, Gill, Henry). But their identity divides the interpretive schools: the faithful witnessing church/ministers, esp. the Waldenses (Gill, historicist); two literal prophets — Enoch and Elijah, or Moses and Elijah (patristic, futurist); the two Testaments (rejected). The verse displays the two-witnesses crux. The verse marks the witnesses: two. The verse displays the debate: the church, or Enoch/Elijah, or the two Testaments. The verse displays the period: 1260 days. The verse displays the dress: sackcloth. The witnesses display the testimony maintained through the long affliction. The verse displays the witnesses: empowered. The verse displays the prophesying: 1260 days. The verse displays the sackcloth: of mourning. The two witnesses prophesying 1260 days in sackcloth display a famous crux: Christ's two witnesses prophesy in sackcloth through the appointed period — read by the historicists as the faithful witnessing church against antichrist (the Waldenses, Gill), by the patristic/futurist tradition as Enoch and Elijah (or Moses and Elijah) returning, and (rejected) as the two Testaments. The verse displays the sackcloth witnesses, the two olive trees and candlesticks (v.4).
4:
"These are the two olive trees, and the two candlesticks standing before the God of the earth." The brief verse notes the witnesses as the two olive trees and candlesticks. The grammatical voices note the figure from Zechariah. Barnes and Robertson note the identification: "these are the two olive trees, and the two candlesticks (lampstands) standing before the God of the earth" — the witnesses are identified with the two olive trees and two lampstands of Zechariah's vision (Zechariah 4); the figure marks them as bearers and channels of light and the Spirit.

On "the two olive trees," the witnesses note the source of oil. Barnes and Ellicott note that in Zechariah the two olive trees supply oil to the lampstand; the witnesses, as olive trees, are filled with and supply the oil of the Spirit; their testimony is sustained by the Spirit's grace, not their own strength ("not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit," Zechariah 4:6).

On "the two candlesticks standing before the God of the earth," the grammatical-historical note the light-bearing: the witnesses are "the two candlesticks (lampstands) standing before the God of the earth" — as lampstands, they hold forth the light of truth before God; the witnessing church/ministers shine as lights in the dark time of apostasy, standing in God's presence. The verse displays the witnesses as the two olive trees and candlesticks.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "these are the two olive trees, and the two candlesticks standing before the God of the earth," the witnesses identified with Zechariah's olive trees and lampstands (Zechariah 4, Barnes, Robertson); "the two olive trees" supplying the oil of the Spirit, the testimony sustained by grace (Zechariah 4:6, Barnes, Ellicott); "the two candlesticks," the lampstands holding forth light before God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the olive trees and lampstands. The verse marks the olive trees: supplying oil. The verse displays the candlesticks: bearing light. The verse displays the source: the Spirit. The verse displays the standing: before God. The figure displays the witnesses light-bearing and Spirit-filled. The verse displays the olives: two. The verse displays the lamps: two. The verse displays the light: borne. The witnesses as the two olive trees and candlesticks display their character: they are the two olive trees and two lampstands of Zechariah's vision — filled with the oil of the Spirit and bearing the light of truth before the God of the earth. The verse displays the Spirit-filled witnesses, armed with power against their enemies (v.5).
5:
"And if any man will hurt them, fire proceedeth out of their mouth, and devoureth their enemies: and if any man will hurt them, he must in this manner be killed." The brief verse notes the witnesses' power to consume their enemies with fire. The grammatical voices note the defensive judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the protection given them: "if any man will hurt them, fire proceedeth out of their mouth, and devoureth their enemies" — the witnesses are divinely protected; any who try to harm them are consumed by fire from their mouth; the fire is the figure of the word of judgment (cf. Jeremiah 5:14; 2 Kings 1:10, Elijah's fire) that destroys their foes.

On "fire proceedeth out of their mouth," the witnesses note the word of judgment. Barnes and JFB note that the "fire out of their mouth" is the judicial power of their testimony — their word brings judgment on persecutors; whether literal fire (in the Elijah-figure) or the consuming power of the word and the judgments it calls down; the witnesses are not defenseless.

On "he must in this manner be killed," the grammatical-historical note the certainty: "if any man will hurt them, he must in this manner be killed" — the doom of their persecutors is certain; whoever would harm them is himself destroyed; the repetition stresses the inviolability of the witnesses during their appointed testimony; they cannot be silenced until their work is done (v.7). The verse displays the witnesses' power to consume their enemies with fire.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the protection — "if any man will hurt them, fire proceedeth out of their mouth, and devoureth their enemies," the witnesses divinely protected, their foes consumed by fire (Elijah, 2 Kings 1:10, Barnes, Robertson); "fire out of their mouth" the judicial power of their testimony, the word that judges persecutors (Jeremiah 5:14, Barnes, JFB); "he must in this manner be killed," the certain doom of persecutors, the witnesses inviolable till their work is done (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the protected witnesses. The verse marks the fire: from their mouth. The verse displays the effect: devours enemies. The verse displays the doom: persecutors killed. The verse displays the inviolability: until their work ends. The power displays the witnesses guarded by consuming judgment. The verse displays the fire: judicial. The verse displays the enemies: devoured. The verse displays the witnesses: inviolable. The witnesses' power to consume their enemies with fire displays their divine protection: any who would harm them are devoured by fire from their mouth — the word of judgment that destroys persecutors, the witnesses inviolable until their testimony is finished. The verse displays the guarded witnesses, with power over heaven and the waters (v.6).
6:
"These have power to shut heaven, that it rain not in the days of their prophecy: and have power over waters to turn them to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues, as often as they will." The brief verse notes the witnesses' Elijah-like and Moses-like powers. The grammatical voices note the prophetic signs. Barnes and Robertson note the powers granted: "these have power to shut heaven, that it rain not in the days of their prophecy: and have power over waters to turn them to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues, as often as they will" — the witnesses wield the powers of Elijah (shutting heaven, 1 Kings 17; James 5:17) and Moses (turning water to blood and smiting with plagues, Exodus 7-12); the figure of prophets armed with divine, judicial power.

On "power to shut heaven, that it rain not," the witnesses note the Elijah-figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that "to shut heaven, that it rain not" recalls Elijah's withholding of rain for three and a half years (1 Kings 17; Luke 4:25) — the same period as the witnesses' testimony; the power to bring drought as judgment.

On "power over waters to turn them to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues," the grammatical-historical note the Moses-figure: the power "to turn waters to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues" recalls Moses' plagues on Egypt (Exodus 7-12) — the power to bring judgment on the persecuting world "as often as they will"; the witnesses, like Moses and Elijah, are armed with judicial power against the ungodly. The verse displays the witnesses' Elijah-like and Moses-like powers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the powers — "power to shut heaven... and power over waters to turn them to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues," the witnesses wielding Elijah's and Moses' powers (Barnes, Robertson); "to shut heaven, that it rain not" Elijah's drought (1 Kings 17; Luke 4:25, Barnes, Ellicott); "turn waters to blood... smite with plagues" Moses' plagues on Egypt (Exodus 7-12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prophetic powers. The verse marks the heaven: shut. The verse displays the waters: turned to blood. The verse displays the plagues: smiting the earth. The verse displays the figures: Elijah and Moses. The powers display the witnesses armed with judicial might. The verse displays the heaven: closed. The verse displays the waters: bloodied. The verse displays the plagues: at will. The witnesses' Elijah-like and Moses-like powers display the judicial might of their testimony: they can shut heaven (Elijah's drought) and turn waters to blood and smite the earth with plagues (Moses' judgments) as often as they will — prophets armed with divine power against the ungodly. The verse displays the powerful witnesses, slain at last by the beast (v.7).
7:
"And when they shall have finished their testimony, the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit shall make war against them, and shall overcome them, and kill them." The witnesses note the beast from the abyss making war on the witnesses and killing them. On "when they shall have finished their testimony," the grammatical voices note the appointed completion. Barnes and Robertson note the timing: "when they shall have finished (completed) their testimony" — the witnesses cannot be killed until their testimony is finished; only when their appointed work is done does their death come; their inviolability (vv.5-6) lasts until the end of their commission.

On "the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit shall make war against them," the witnesses note the persecuting power. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit (the abyss)" — the first mention of "the beast," the great persecuting power (later described in chs. 13, 17); in the historicist reading, the antichristian/papal power; it "makes war" against the witnesses, the organized hostility of the persecuting power against the faithful.

On "shall overcome them, and kill them," the grammatical-historical note the apparent defeat: the beast "shall overcome them, and kill them" — the witnesses are at last overcome and slain; the testimony is, for a time, silenced and seemingly defeated; the temporary triumph of the persecuting power over the witnessing church. The verse displays the beast from the abyss making war on the witnesses and killing them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the timing — "when they shall have finished their testimony," the witnesses unkillable until their appointed work is done (Barnes, Robertson); "the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit shall make war against them," the great persecuting power (the beast, chs. 13, 17), the antichristian power to historicists, warring on the witnesses (Barnes, Ellicott); "shall overcome them, and kill them," the witnesses overcome and slain, the testimony seemingly defeated (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the slain witnesses. The verse marks the testimony: finished. The verse displays the beast: from the abyss. The verse displays the war: against the witnesses. The verse displays the defeat: overcome and killed. The vision displays the persecuting power slaying the witnesses. The verse displays the testimony: completed. The verse displays the beast: warring. The verse displays the witnesses: killed. The beast from the abyss making war on the witnesses and killing them displays the apparent triumph of the persecutor: only when the witnesses have finished their testimony does the beast from the abyss make war on them, overcome them, and kill them — the witnessing church for a time silenced. The verse displays the slain witnesses, whose bodies lie unburied (v.8).
8:
"And their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city, which spiritually is called Sodom and Egypt, where also our Lord was crucified." The witnesses note the witnesses' bodies in the street of the great city. On "their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city," the grammatical voices note the unburied corpses. Barnes and Robertson note the indignity: "their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city" — the slain witnesses are left unburied, exposed in the public street; the refusal of burial (v.9) is the height of contempt and triumph over them.

On "which spiritually is called Sodom and Egypt," the witnesses note the symbolic name. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the great city, which spiritually (figuratively, allegorically) is called Sodom and Egypt" — the city is named, not literally, but spiritually: "Sodom" (for its wickedness and immorality) and "Egypt" (for its oppression and bondage of God's people); the apostate, persecuting city/power is meant; the historicists read it of Rome (the great city of ch. 17), the seat of the antichristian power.

On "where also our Lord was crucified," interpreters note the reference. Barnes and Gill note that "where also our Lord was crucified" most naturally recalls Jerusalem (where Christ was crucified); but on the symbolic/historicist reading, it means the city/power that is, in spirit, like the Jerusalem that crucified Christ — the persecuting power that crucifies Christ afresh in his members; the great apostate city that slays the witnesses as Jerusalem slew the Lord. The verse displays the witnesses' bodies in the street of the great city.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the indignity — "their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city," the slain witnesses left unburied in contempt (Barnes, Robertson); "spiritually... called Sodom and Egypt," the apostate persecuting city named for wickedness and oppression, Rome to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "where also our Lord was crucified," recalling Jerusalem, or the power that crucifies Christ in his members (Barnes, Gill). The verse displays the great city. The verse marks the bodies: in the street. The verse displays the name: Sodom and Egypt. The verse displays the meaning: the apostate city. The verse displays the reference: where Christ was crucified. The vision displays the witnesses dishonored in the persecuting city. The verse displays the bodies: unburied. The verse displays the city: Sodom, Egypt. The verse displays the Lord: crucified there. The witnesses' bodies in the street of the great city display the indignity of their apparent defeat: their corpses lie unburied in the street of the great city — spiritually called Sodom and Egypt, where the Lord was crucified — the apostate, persecuting power that slays the witnesses as Jerusalem slew Christ. The verse displays the unburied witnesses, gloated over by the nations (vv.9-10).
9:
"And they of the people and kindreds and tongues and nations shall see their dead bodies three days and an half, and shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves." The brief verse notes the witnesses' bodies viewed and denied burial three and a half days. The grammatical voices note the public exposure. Barnes and Robertson note the universal gaze: "they of the people and kindreds and tongues and nations shall see their dead bodies three days and an half" — peoples from all nations look upon the unburied corpses for three and a half days; the slain witnesses are exposed to the gaze of the whole hostile world; the fourfold designation marks the universal scope of the spectacle.

On "three days and an half," the witnesses note the limited period. Barnes and Ellicott note that "three days and an half" is the period of the witnesses' apparent death — a short, limited time; in the historicist reading, by the day-year principle, three and a half years (a brief period corresponding to the years the witnesses lay "dead"); the short duration of their seeming defeat.

On "shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves," the grammatical-historical note the contempt: the enemies "shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves" — denying burial was the ultimate insult and triumph; the refusal of a grave expresses the malice and exultation of the persecutors over the silenced witnesses; the bodies left exposed in scorn. The verse displays the witnesses' bodies viewed and denied burial three and a half days.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exposure — "they of the people and kindreds and tongues and nations shall see their dead bodies three days and an half," the corpses viewed by all nations for the brief period (Barnes, Robertson); "three days and an half" the limited time of their apparent death, three and a half years to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves," the denial of burial the ultimate contempt and triumph (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the exposed witnesses. The verse marks the viewing: by all nations. The verse displays the period: three and a half days. The verse displays the denial: of burial. The verse displays the contempt: of the persecutors. The exposure displays the witnesses scorned and unburied before the world. The verse displays the bodies: viewed. The verse displays the days: three and a half. The verse displays the graves: denied. The witnesses' bodies viewed and denied burial three and a half days display the depth of their apparent defeat: peoples of all nations gaze on the unburied corpses for three and a half days, denying them a grave — the contempt and triumph of the persecutors over the silenced witnesses. The verse displays the scorned witnesses, over whom the world rejoices (v.10).
10:
"And they that dwell upon the earth shall rejoice over them, and make merry, and shall send gifts one to another; because these two prophets tormented them that dwelt on the earth." The brief verse notes the world's rejoicing over the dead witnesses. The grammatical voices note the exultation. Barnes and Robertson note the celebration: "they that dwell upon the earth shall rejoice over them, and make merry, and shall send gifts one to another" — the earth-dwellers (the ungodly world) rejoice and celebrate over the death of the witnesses, exchanging gifts as in a festival; the world is glad to be rid of the troublesome witnesses.

On "shall send gifts one to another," the witnesses note the festivity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the exchange of gifts marks a time of public rejoicing and festival (cf. Esther 9:22; Nehemiah 8:10) — the world treats the witnesses' death as cause for celebration; the contrast between the world's joy and the witnesses' apparent defeat heightens the irony before their resurrection.

On "because these two prophets tormented them that dwelt on the earth," the puritan Henry notes the reason: the world rejoices "because these two prophets tormented (vexed, troubled) them that dwelt on the earth" — the witnesses' testimony had been a torment to the ungodly; their faithful witness, rebuke, and the judgments they called down vexed the world's conscience; the world is relieved at their silencing. The verse displays the world's rejoicing over the dead witnesses.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the celebration — "they that dwell upon the earth shall rejoice over them, and make merry, and shall send gifts one to another," the ungodly world celebrating the witnesses' death as a festival (Barnes, Robertson); "send gifts one to another" the mark of public rejoicing (Esther 9:22, Barnes, Ellicott); "because these two prophets tormented them," the witnesses' testimony having vexed the world's conscience (Henry). The verse displays the world's joy. The verse marks the rejoicing: of the earth-dwellers. The verse displays the festivity: gifts exchanged. The verse displays the reason: the prophets tormented them. The verse displays the relief: at their silencing. The exultation displays the world glad to be rid of the witnesses. The verse displays the rejoicing: festive. The verse displays the gifts: exchanged. The verse displays the torment: ended. The world's rejoicing over the dead witnesses displays the ungodly's triumph: the earth-dwellers rejoice, make merry, and exchange gifts over the witnesses' death — glad to be rid of the prophets whose testimony had tormented their conscience. The verse displays the world's brief joy, ended by the witnesses' resurrection (v.11).
11:
"And after three days and an half the Spirit of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet; and great fear fell upon them which saw them." The witnesses note the witnesses raised by the Spirit of life. On "after three days and an half the Spirit of life from God entered into them," the grammatical voices note the resurrection. Barnes and Robertson note the reversal: "after three days and an half the Spirit (breath) of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet" — when the brief period of their death ends, the Spirit of life from God enters the slain witnesses (echoing Ezekiel 37:10, the dry bones); they are raised and stand on their feet; the testimony, seemingly dead, is revived by God's power.

On "the Spirit of life from God entered into them," the witnesses note the divine power. Barnes and Ellicott note that the raising is God's act — "the Spirit of life from God" — not human; the witnessing church/testimony, which the world thought destroyed, is restored to life by God; the resurrection of the witnesses (whether of literal prophets or, in the historicist reading, the revival of the witnessing church, e.g. at the Reformation).

On "great fear fell upon them which saw them," the grammatical-historical note the terror of the world: "great fear fell upon them which saw them" — the world's rejoicing turns to terror at the sight of the revived witnesses; the same enemies who exulted now tremble; the reversal strikes fear into the persecutors. The verse displays the witnesses raised by the Spirit of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resurrection — "after three days and an half the Spirit of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet," the slain witnesses raised by God's breath (Ezekiel 37, Barnes, Robertson); "the Spirit of life from God" the divine act, the testimony restored, the witnessing church revived (Barnes, Ellicott); "great fear fell upon them which saw them," the world's joy turned to terror at the revived witnesses (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the raised witnesses. The verse marks the time: after three and a half days. The verse displays the Spirit: of life. The verse displays the rising: stood on their feet. The verse displays the fear: on the beholders. The resurrection displays the witnesses revived and the world terrified. The verse displays the Spirit: entering. The verse displays the witnesses: risen. The verse displays the fear: great. The witnesses raised by the Spirit of life display the reversal of their apparent defeat: after the three and a half days, the Spirit of life from God enters them and they stand on their feet, and great fear falls on those who see them — the testimony revived, the world's triumph turned to terror. The verse displays the revived witnesses, called up to heaven (v.12).
12:
"And they heard a great voice from heaven saying unto them, Come up hither. And they ascended up to heaven in a cloud; and their enemies beheld them." The brief verse notes the witnesses called up to heaven before their enemies. The grammatical voices note the ascension. Barnes and Robertson note the exaltation: "they heard a great voice from heaven saying unto them, Come up hither. And they ascended up to heaven in a cloud" — the witnesses, raised, are summoned by a heavenly voice to ascend; they go up to heaven in a cloud (as Christ ascended, Acts 1:9; as Elijah was taken up); the full vindication and exaltation of the witnesses.

On "Come up hither," the witnesses note the heavenly call. Barnes and Ellicott note that the call "Come up hither" is the summons to glory — the witnesses are not merely revived but exalted to heaven; their reward and triumph after their testimony and death; the seemingly defeated are openly honored.

On "their enemies beheld them," the grammatical-historical note the public vindication: "their enemies beheld them" — the witnesses' ascension is seen by their enemies; the very persecutors who exulted over their death now witness their triumphant exaltation; the open vindication before those who had despised them; the reversal is complete and public. The verse displays the witnesses called up to heaven before their enemies.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ascension — "Come up hither. And they ascended up to heaven in a cloud," the witnesses summoned and exalted to heaven in a cloud, as Christ and Elijah (Acts 1:9, Barnes, Robertson); "Come up hither" the summons to glory, the witnesses exalted and rewarded (Barnes, Ellicott); "their enemies beheld them," the public vindication before the persecutors who had exulted (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the exalted witnesses. The verse marks the call: come up hither. The verse displays the ascension: in a cloud. The verse displays the witnesses: exalted. The verse displays the enemies: beholding. The ascension displays the witnesses vindicated before their foes. The verse displays the voice: from heaven. The verse displays the cloud: of ascent. The verse displays the enemies: watching. The witnesses called up to heaven before their enemies display the full vindication of the testimony: a heavenly voice bids them "Come up hither," and they ascend in a cloud while their enemies behold them — the seemingly defeated witnesses openly exalted before those who despised them. The verse displays the ascended witnesses, vindicated by an earthquake (v.13).
13:
"And the same hour was there a great earthquake, and the tenth part of the city fell, and in the earthquake were slain of men seven thousand: and the remnant were affrighted, and gave glory to the God of heaven." The witnesses note the earthquake and the remnant giving glory to God. On "the same hour was there a great earthquake, and the tenth part of the city fell," the grammatical voices note the judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the convulsion accompanying the ascension: "the same hour was there a great earthquake, and the tenth part of the city fell" — at the moment of the witnesses' vindication a great earthquake strikes, and a tenth of the great city collapses; the figure of a great judgment or revolution shaking the persecuting power; in the historicist reading, a political/religious convulsion.

On "in the earthquake were slain of men seven thousand," the witnesses note the slain. Barnes and Ellicott note that "seven thousand (literally, seven thousand names of men)" were slain in the earthquake — a definite number; whether literal or symbolic, a notable destruction; the judgment falls on the city that had triumphed over the witnesses.

On "the remnant were affrighted, and gave glory to the God of heaven," the grammatical-historical note the response: "the remnant were affrighted, and gave glory to the God of heaven" — those who survived the judgment were terrified and gave glory to God; whether in true repentance or forced acknowledgment, the survivors are brought to own God's hand; a notable, hopeful result — some are turned to give God glory (contrast 9:20-21, where none repented). The verse displays the earthquake and the remnant giving glory to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the judgment — "a great earthquake, and the tenth part of the city fell," a great convulsion shaking the persecuting power, a political/religious upheaval to historicists (Barnes, Robertson); "slain of men seven thousand," a definite, notable destruction (Barnes, Ellicott); "the remnant were affrighted, and gave glory to the God of heaven," the survivors brought to own God's hand, some turned to give him glory (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the shaking judgment. The verse marks the earthquake: great. The verse displays the city: a tenth fell. The verse displays the slain: seven thousand. The verse displays the remnant: gave glory. The judgment displays the persecuting power shaken and survivors humbled. The verse displays the earthquake: convulsing. The verse displays the city: fallen. The verse displays the glory: given to God. The earthquake and the remnant giving glory to God display the judgment that vindicates the witnesses: a great earthquake topples a tenth of the city and slays seven thousand, and the terrified remnant give glory to the God of heaven — the persecuting power shaken and some brought to own God. The verse displays the glorifying remnant, closing the second woe (v.14).
14:
"The second woe is past; and, behold, the third woe cometh quickly." The brief verse notes the passing of the second woe and the coming of the third. The grammatical voices note the structuring announcement. Barnes and Robertson note the transition (as at 9:12): "the second woe is past; and, behold, the third woe cometh quickly" — the sixth trumpet (the second woe, including the Euphratean horsemen and the interlude of the witnesses) is now past; the third woe (the seventh trumpet) is announced as coming quickly; the marker between the trumpets.

On "the second woe is past," the witnesses note the completed judgment. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the second woe is past" closes the long section of the sixth trumpet (chs. 9-11); the second of the three woe-trumpets (8:13) has run its course; the announcement prepares for the seventh.

On "the third woe cometh quickly," the puritan Henry notes the imminence: "the third woe cometh quickly" — the seventh trumpet, the third and final woe, is now near; "quickly" marks its swift approach; yet the seventh trumpet, when it sounds (v.15), brings not only woe but the consummation and the kingdom of Christ; the final woe and the final triumph are bound together. The verse displays the passing of the second woe and the coming of the third.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the transition — "the second woe is past; and, behold, the third woe cometh quickly," the sixth trumpet past, the seventh (third woe) coming quickly (9:12, Barnes, Robertson); "the second woe is past" closing the sixth-trumpet section (chs. 9-11, Barnes, Ellicott); "the third woe cometh quickly," the seventh trumpet near, bringing both woe and the kingdom (Henry). The verse displays the woe-marker. The verse marks the second woe: past. The verse displays the third woe: coming. The verse displays the imminence: quickly. The verse displays the structure: the three woes. The announcement displays the second woe ended, the final one near. The verse displays the woe: passed. The verse displays the third: coming. The verse displays the quickness: imminent. The passing of the second woe and the coming of the third display the structuring marker before the seventh trumpet: the second woe is past, and the third woe comes quickly — the seventh and final trumpet, which brings both the last woe and the consummation of Christ's kingdom. The verse displays the coming third woe, sounded by the seventh angel (v.15).
15:
"And the seventh angel sounded; and there were great voices in heaven, saying, The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ; and he shall reign for ever and ever." The witnesses note the seventh trumpet and the kingdoms of the world becoming Christ's. On "the seventh angel sounded; and there were great voices in heaven," the grammatical voices note the climactic trumpet. Barnes and Robertson note the sounding of the last trumpet: "the seventh angel sounded; and there were great (loud) voices in heaven" — the seventh and final trumpet sounds, and heavenly voices proclaim the great consummation; the third woe is also the announcement of the triumph of God's kingdom.

On "the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ," the witnesses note the great proclamation. Barnes and Ellicott note the announcement (some texts: "the kingdom of the world is become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ") — the sovereignty over the world has passed to God and his Christ; the long-contested dominion of the world is now Christ's; the goal of the whole book proleptically declared — the universal reign of Christ.

On "he shall reign for ever and ever," the eastern and grammatical-historical note the eternal reign: "he shall reign for ever and ever" — the reign of Christ (or of God) is everlasting; the kingdom now established shall never end; the consummation of all things in the unending reign of the Lord and his Christ. The verse displays the seventh trumpet and the kingdoms of the world becoming Christ's.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the climactic trumpet — "the seventh angel sounded; and there were great voices in heaven," the final trumpet and the heavenly proclamation of the consummation (Barnes, Robertson); "the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ," the world's sovereignty passed to Christ, the goal of the book declared (Barnes, Ellicott); "he shall reign for ever and ever," the everlasting reign of Christ, the kingdom never ending (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the kingdom of Christ. The verse marks the trumpet: the seventh. The verse displays the proclamation: the kingdoms are Christ's. The verse displays the dominion: passed to the Lord. The verse displays the reign: for ever. The trumpet displays the world's kingdom become Christ's eternal reign. The verse displays the trumpet: sounded. The verse displays the kingdoms: Christ's. The verse displays the reign: everlasting. The seventh trumpet and the kingdoms of the world becoming Christ's display the climax of the trumpet-series: at the seventh trumpet, heavenly voices proclaim that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, who shall reign forever — the great consummation declared. The verse displays Christ's eternal reign, worshipped by the elders (vv.16-17).
16:
"And the four and twenty elders, which sat before God on their seats, fell upon their faces, and worshipped God," The brief verse notes the twenty-four elders worshipping God at the proclamation. The grammatical voices note the response of heaven. Barnes and Robertson note the worship: "the four and twenty elders, which sat before God on their seats (thrones), fell upon their faces, and worshipped God" — the twenty-four elders (representing the redeemed church, introduced in ch. 4) respond to the proclamation of Christ's kingdom by falling on their faces in worship; the heavenly host adores God for the establishment of his reign.

On "the four and twenty elders," the witnesses note their identity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "twenty-four elders" (the heavenly representatives of God's people, perhaps the twelve patriarchs and twelve apostles, or the whole redeemed church) lead the worship; they sit on thrones before God, sharing his reign, and now bow in adoration.

On "fell upon their faces, and worshipped God," the puritan Henry notes the posture of adoration: the elders "fell upon their faces, and worshipped God" — the lowliest posture of reverence and praise; at the announcement of the kingdom, heaven responds not with self-congratulation but with humble worship of God; the consummation calls forth adoration. The verse displays the twenty-four elders worshipping God at the proclamation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the worship — "the four and twenty elders... fell upon their faces, and worshipped God," the elders responding to the kingdom-proclamation with worship (Barnes, Robertson); the "twenty-four elders" the heavenly representatives of the redeemed church, sharing God's reign (ch. 4, Barnes, Ellicott); "fell upon their faces, and worshipped God," the lowly posture of adoration, heaven worshipping at the consummation (Henry). The verse displays the worshipping elders. The verse marks the elders: twenty-four. The verse displays the thrones: before God. The verse displays the posture: on their faces. The verse displays the response: worship. The worship displays heaven adoring God for his kingdom. The verse displays the elders: bowing. The verse displays the faces: prostrate. The verse displays the worship: of God. The twenty-four elders worshipping God at the proclamation display heaven's response to the consummation: the elders, seated before God on their thrones, fall on their faces and worship God at the announcement of his kingdom. The verse displays the worshipping elders, giving thanks for God's reign (v.17).
17:
"Saying, We give thee thanks, O Lord God Almighty, which art, and wast, and art to come; because thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned." The witnesses note the elders' thanksgiving that God has taken his power and reigned. On "We give thee thanks, O Lord God Almighty, which art, and wast, and art to come," the grammatical voices note the address. Barnes and Robertson note the elders' praise: "We give thee thanks, O Lord God Almighty, which art, and wast, and art to come" — the elders give thanks to the eternal, almighty God (the title from 1:4, 8); they praise the everliving God for the establishment of his reign.

On "thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned," the witnesses note the assumption of the reign. Barnes and Ellicott note the cause of thanks: "thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned (begun to reign)" — God has now openly assumed his great power and begun to reign; the kingdom long awaited is now established; God, who always reigned in providence, now reigns manifestly and triumphantly over the world; the consummation of his kingdom.

On the omission of "art to come," the grammatical-historical note a textual point: many texts read only "which art, and wast" (omitting "art to come") — fittingly, for at the consummation God is no longer the "coming" one but the one who has now come and reigns; the reign so long future is now present; the praise marks the arrival of the kingdom. The verse displays the elders' thanksgiving that God has taken his power and reigned.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the thanksgiving — "We give thee thanks, O Lord God Almighty, which art, and wast," the elders praising the eternal almighty God (1:8, Barnes, Robertson); "thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned," God openly assuming his power and beginning to reign, the kingdom established (Barnes, Ellicott); the omission of "art to come" fitting the consummation, God no longer coming but reigning (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the thanksgiving. The verse marks the thanks: to the Almighty. The verse displays the title: art and wast. The verse displays the power: taken. The verse displays the reign: begun. The praise displays the elders thanking God for his manifest reign. The verse displays the thanks: given. The verse displays the power: assumed. The verse displays the reign: established. The elders' thanksgiving that God has taken his power and reigned displays heaven's praise at the consummation: the elders give thanks to the Lord God Almighty, who is and was, because he has taken his great power and begun to reign — the kingdom long awaited now established. The verse displays the reigning God, judging the nations and rewarding the saints (v.18).
18:
"And the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come, and the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and that thou shouldest give reward unto thy servants the prophets, and to the saints, and them that fear thy name, small and great; and shouldest destroy them which destroy the earth." The witnesses note the time of wrath, judgment, reward, and the destruction of the destroyers. On "the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come," the grammatical voices note the conflict of wraths. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "the nations were angry (enraged against God and his kingdom), and thy wrath is come" — the nations rage against God's reign, but God's wrath has now come; the angry rebellion of the nations is met by the righteous wrath of God; the time of reckoning (echoing Psalm 2).

On "the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and that thou shouldest give reward unto thy servants," the witnesses note the judgment and reward. Barnes and Ellicott note that the seventh trumpet brings "the time of the dead, that they should be judged (the general judgment), and that thou shouldest give reward unto thy servants the prophets, and to the saints, and them that fear thy name, small and great" — the time of judging the dead and rewarding God's faithful servants (prophets, saints, all who fear God, great and small) has come; the consummation includes the judgment and the recompense of the righteous.

On "shouldest destroy them which destroy the earth," the puritan Henry notes the doom of the destroyers: "shouldest destroy them which destroy (corrupt, ruin) the earth" — the time has come for God to destroy those who destroyed the earth (the persecutors, corrupters, and oppressors); the just retribution on the enemies of God and his people; the kingdom's coming brings both reward for the faithful and ruin for the destroyers. The verse displays the time of wrath, judgment, reward, and the destruction of the destroyers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conflict — "the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come," the raging nations met by God's righteous wrath (Psalm 2, Barnes, Robertson); "the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and... give reward unto thy servants," the judgment of the dead and the reward of the faithful (Barnes, Ellicott); "destroy them which destroy the earth," the doom of the corrupters and persecutors (Henry). The verse displays the consummation. The verse marks the nations: angry. The verse displays the wrath: come. The verse displays the judgment: of the dead. The verse displays the reward: to the saints. The verse displays the destruction: of the destroyers. The consummation displays wrath, judgment, reward, and retribution. The verse displays the wrath: come. The verse displays the reward: given. The verse displays the destroyers: destroyed. The time of wrath, judgment, reward, and the destruction of the destroyers displays the full content of the seventh trumpet: the nations rage but God's wrath has come — the time to judge the dead, to reward his servants the prophets and saints, small and great, and to destroy those who destroy the earth. The verse displays the consummating judgment, sealed by the opened heavenly temple (v.19).
19:
"And the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament: and there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail." The witnesses note the heavenly temple opened, revealing the ark, with mighty signs. On "the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament," the grammatical voices note the opened sanctuary. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament (covenant)" — the heavenly temple is opened, revealing the ark of the covenant within; the ark, the symbol of God's covenant presence and faithfulness (hidden in the earthly Holy of Holies), is now disclosed in heaven; the figure of God's covenant mercy and presence made manifest at the consummation.

On "the ark of his testament," the witnesses note the covenant. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the ark of his testament (covenant)" signifies God's covenant with his people — his faithfulness, mercy, and the sure fulfillment of his promises; the opening of the temple and the seeing of the ark assure the redeemed of God's covenant kept; the long-hidden ark revealed marks the access and the faithfulness of God.

On "there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail," the grammatical-historical note the theophany: the opened temple is accompanied by "lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail" — the tokens of divine majesty and judgment (cf. Exodus 19; 8:5; 16:18); the awe-inspiring signs that attend God's presence and the consummating judgment; the chapter closes on the majesty and terror of the divine appearing. The verse displays the heavenly temple opened, revealing the ark, with mighty signs.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the opened temple — "the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament," the heavenly sanctuary opened, the ark of the covenant revealed (Barnes, Robertson); "the ark of his testament" the symbol of God's covenant faithfulness and mercy, his promises kept (Barnes, Ellicott); "lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail," the tokens of divine majesty and judgment (Exodus 19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the opened sanctuary. The verse marks the temple: opened in heaven. The verse displays the ark: of the covenant. The verse displays the meaning: God's faithfulness. The verse displays the signs: majesty and judgment. The vision displays God's covenant presence revealed in majesty. The verse displays the temple: opened. The verse displays the ark: seen. The verse displays the signs: mighty. The heavenly temple opened, revealing the ark, with mighty signs, displays the majestic close of the trumpet-series: the temple of God in heaven is opened, the ark of his covenant is seen, and lightnings, voices, thunders, an earthquake, and great hail attend — God's covenant faithfulness revealed amid the tokens of his majesty and judgment. The verse displays the opened temple, closing the seventh trumpet and opening the great signs of the woman and the dragon (ch.12).
